title: HELOC vs Refinance: Which Is Right for You?
metaTitle: HELOC vs Refinance Canada — Which to Choose & When
metaDescription: HELOC or refinance? Honest comparison of when each makes sense for Canadian homeowners — including the math, costs and break-even analysis.
excerpt: Both unlock home equity. They work very differently. Here's how to decide between a HELOC and a refinance.

body.heading: The core difference
paragraphs: A refinance replaces your existing mortgage with a new, larger one — pulling equity out as a one-time lump sum. A HELOC is a separate product registered behind (or alongside) your mortgage, giving you revolving credit access at variable rates with interest-only minimum payments.
Refinance: fixed amount, fixed-payment schedule, lower rate. Best when you have a specific large use case (consolidation, renovation, second-property down payment) and want predictable payments.
HELOC: revolving access, interest-only minimum, variable rate. Best when you want emergency reserve, opportunistic access to capital, or are smoothing self-employed income.

body.heading: The penalty math
paragraphs: Refinances usually break your existing mortgage early — triggering a prepayment penalty. On a big-bank fixed-rate mortgage that penalty can be $20,000–$40,000. A HELOC adds new credit without breaking the existing mortgage, so no penalty applies. For Greater Vancouver homeowners with low-rate fixed mortgages from 2020–2021, a HELOC is often dramatically cheaper to set up than a refinance.

body.heading: When to use both
paragraphs: Many of my clients end up with a refinance plus a HELOC: refinance the mortgage to a better rate or longer amortization, and add a HELOC behind it for ongoing flexibility. The combined product is registered as one charge against the property; you get the predictability of a mortgage and the flexibility of a credit line.

body.heading: Common scenarios and which wins
bullets: Consolidating $50K of credit-card debt: Refinance — fixed payment, lower rate, structured paydown.
Renovating $80K of kitchen and bathrooms: Refinance with PPI on a new build, or HELOC on an existing property.
Buying a $1.2M rental needing $300K down: HELOC on existing home (faster, cheaper than refinance).
Emergency reserve: HELOC — only pay interest when you actually use it.
Lower the rate on an existing balance: Refinance (HELOC rates are higher than mortgage rates).

faqs.question: Can I have a HELOC and a mortgage at the same lender?
faqs.answer: Yes — and many lenders offer combined products that streamline this. Total combined LTV is capped at 80%, with up to 65% on the HELOC portion.

faqs.question: Are HELOC interest rates negotiable?
faqs.answer: Slightly. Most are priced at prime + 0.5% to prime + 1.0%. Stronger files (high credit, low LTV, established relationship) can negotiate to the lower end of that range.
